dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for breathing problems.
Hello, I'm EMT Taylor. Can I ask what brought you to call 911 today?
I have asthma; I've been short of breath all day. My nebulizer isn't helping.
Can you tell me the date today, and where we are?
My name is Evelyn, and today is October 2nd. We're in [CITY].
Can you tell me what you're planning to do today before we started feeling unwell?
I was just going to rest, but I started getting more winded.
Great. Evelyn, your skin doesn't look pale, and you're not sweaty, which is a good sign.
Evelyn, we're going to check a few things to help understand what's going on with your breathing.
Evelyn, can you tell me if you're comfortable with us checking your lungs?
Sure, go ahead.
Loud bilateral wheezes. That's pretty concerning for an asthma flare-up.
Evelyn, we're going to check how fast you're breathing now. Okay?
Your respiratory rate is 24-very fast and labored.
Have you found it hard to take full breaths at all today?
Yes, every breath is difficult. It's been like this all day.
Evelyn, we'll now check your pulse.
Strong and regular pulse-currently at 101 beats per minute, which is fast.
Evelyn, your skin doesn't appear pale or sweaty, which is reassuring.
Your blood pressure is 151 over 92.
Just a quick check of your finger-I can see your oxygen level is at 94. That's lower than normal but not severe.
Are you experiencing chest pain or abdominal discomfort?
No. Only shortness of breath.
What medications do you usually take, Evelyn?
Only albuterol. I've used it four times already today.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. -> events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: When did the breathing trouble first become noticeable?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. -> events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: All day. I tried my nebulizer three times, but no improvement.
Evelyn, we're going to perform a quick EKG to check your heart now. We also need to run a 4-lead monitoring.
The EKG pattern shows sinus tachycardia-this isn't dangerous, but it's outside your normal rhythm.
We're going to start a nebulizer treatment to assist with your breathing. You okay with that?
It'll be a combination of albuterol and ipratropium in the nebulizer. This should help open your airways.
We're also giving you oxygen at 8 liters a minute. It should help with your shortness of breath.
Evelyn, would it be okay if we placed some wires on your chest and back to monitor more closely?
I'll assess the EKG now and keep checking the rhythm from here.
We are following protocol for respiratory distress due to asthma.
Your breathing has picked up slightly-now at 25. Still pretty high.
Your blood pressure is up slightly to 158 over 71 now.
Oxygen saturation is now 93. Small drop but still manageable.
Your heart rhythm normalized to sinus rhythm, so that's a good sign.
Evelyn, do you currently have any pain?
No, I do not have any pain.
Evelyn, we're going to also give you dexamethasone through your IV. This will help reduce swelling in your airways.
We've started an IV in your arm for medication use, if needed. It's functional and secure.
Evelyn, would you like to be taken to a specific hospital for treatment?
[COMPANY_NAME], please. I'm familiar with the staff there.
We'll be moving you to the stretcher, Evelyn. It's safest with a stair chair. Consenting?
Secured on the stretcher with straps and rails. All set for transport.
Just a quick check, Evelyn. Your blood sugar is reading zero. We'll keep an eye on that.
Evelyn, you remain alert and oriented-person, place, time, and situation-which is normal.
Evelyn, we just checked your end-tidal CO2. It is normal but with an abnormal waveform indicative of bronchoconstriction.
You're not showing any signs of sweating or pale skin, which is very reassuring.
Your Glasgow Coma Scale is 15-eye 4, verbal 5, motor 6-fully responsive and normal.
Evelyn, transport is ready. We'll head to [COMPANY_NAME] for full care.
Receiving a 46-year-old female with an active asthma exacerbation. She has used albuterol four times with no improvement.
Vital signs are trending stable. IV is placed and dexamethasone has been administered. Patient was alert and oriented (AOx4), with no reported pain and not diaphoretic.
The patient's glucose is currently at zero. We also observed normal end-tidal CO2 with an abnormal waveform. We've documented all interventions and are transporting to ED 13 at [COMPANY_NAME].
